description:
You are an observability engineer who has been asked to prep a **single, tidy CSV file** that will be fed directly into a Grafana dashboard.  
Two raw “pipe-delimited” log files have already been collected for you in the directory  

    /home/user/observability/

1.  File: /home/user/observability/metrics_cpu.log  
    • Field order: timestamp | host | cpu_usage_percent | load_average | context_switches  

2.  File: /home/user/observability/metrics_mem.log  
    • Field order: timestamp | host | mem_used_mb | mem_free_mb | swap_used_mb  

Goal  
Create a **new** file called  

    /home/user/observability/dashboard_source.csv  

whose comma-separated columns appear **exactly** in the following order and naming (including the header row):  

    timestamp,host,cpu_usage_percent,mem_used_mb

Rules & checks the automated grader will run  
a. dashboard_source.csv MUST exist at the path above.  
b. dashboard_source.csv MUST contain **six** lines (one header + five data rows).  
c. Lines MUST be terminated by a single UNIX newline character “\n”.  
d. The five data rows must match the corresponding rows from the two source files so that timestamp and host remain perfectly aligned.  
e. A helper log called  

       /home/user/observability/verification.log  

   must be produced. It must contain exactly one line:  

       total_records=5  

   (the number of *data* rows, not counting the header). No additional whitespace.

Technical constraints  
• Use standard GNU/Linux command-line utilities only (for instance, cut, paste, awk, etc.).  
• Do **not** edit the two source log files.  
• Do **not** sort or reorder rows—keep the original order intact.  
• You have normal user privileges only; stay within /home/user.  

Tip  
The quickest approach is to cut the required columns from each file in parallel then paste them side-by-side before converting the delimiter to a comma. Any reproducible method that satisfies the checks above is acceptable.  

When you are done, you should be able to:

    cat /home/user/observability/dashboard_source.csv
    cat /home/user/observability/verification.log

and obtain exactly the formats described.
truth:
Initial filesystem state (already present before the agent starts):

/home/user/observability/metrics_cpu.log  – contents:
timestamp|host|cpu_usage_percent|load_average|context_switches
2024-12-01T12:00:00Z|server-a|37.5|0.89|2312
2024-12-01T12:05:00Z|server-a|42.1|0.92|2478

2024-12-01T12:20:00Z|server-a|41.8|0.93|2505

/home/user/observability/metrics_mem.log  – contents:
timestamp|host|mem_used_mb|mem_free_mb|swap_used_mb
2024-12-01T12:00:00Z|server-a|5678|1024|256
2024-12-01T12:05:00Z|server-a|5690|1012|256
2024-12-01T12:10:00Z|server-a|5705|997|256
2024-12-01T12:15:00Z|server-a|5710|992|256
2024-12-01T12:20:00Z|server-a|5725|977|256

Desired final state after the agent finishes:

1. /home/user/observability/dashboard_source.csv – exact contents (each line ends with “\n”):
timestamp,host,cpu_usage_percent,mem_used_mb
2024-12-01T12:00:00Z,server-a,37.5,5678
2024-12-01T12:05:00Z,server-a,42.1,5690
2024-12-01T12:10:00Z,server-a,45.0,5705
2024-12-01T12:15:00Z,server-a,39.2,5710
2024-12-01T12:20:00Z,server-a,41.8,5725

2. /home/user/observability/verification.log – exact contents:
total_records=5\n
name: task_000000_aeb8b2b7
